pressure_source: twenty-five years of accumulated grudges compressed into one evening
state_shift: Martin is found dead — the ritual weekend becomes a crime scene
must_land_beats: Establish all seven men and their group dynamics through the draft
Martin's controlling generosity and quiet authority over the group
Tommy gives Martin the Viagra as a running joke
Joel's visible anger that no one can identify the source of
Nate records portions of the evening for his podcast
Cliff makes pointed comments about money Martin owes him
Doug's quiet self-deprecation and tendency to disappear into the background
Nate discovers Martin dead at chapter's end
secondary_character_beats: Doug should have a moment of genuine warmth that establishes him as someone who came to this weekend because he needed people
Cliff's deal-making language in the draft should feel like second nature, not a villainous tell

setups_to_plant.description: Tommy gives Martin a Viagra pill as a joke about Martin's age — played for laughs, with no hint it matters medically
Joel and Martin argue heatedly late at night; Joel refuses to explain to anyone what it was about
Nate records portions of draft night on his phone for podcast content — ambient audio that will later prove evidentially relevant
Subtle moments of Martin being unusually protective or deferential to Tommy — making sure Tommy's glass is full, including Tommy in conversations Tommy tries to leave, a private word on the deck

pressure_source: five men independently deciding to tamper with the same death scene
state_shift: a natural death now looks like a murder designed by a committee that never met
must_land_beats: Paul finds Martin and attempts CPR — genuine desperation, not procedure
Paul's irrational decision to reposition the body and wipe surfaces
Paul hears others moving through the house during the night but doesn't know what they're doing
Dawn reveals a scene that looks nothing like what Paul found at 2 AM
The collective effect: five independent panic attacks producing what looks like coordinated murder
secondary_character_beats: Paul's grief for Martin should be visceral and specific — this is his best friend of twenty-five years, not an abstraction
The sounds Paul hears from other rooms (footsteps, a flush, a door) should be specific enough to become identifiable in retrospect

pressure_source: Tommy's conviction that the Viagra he gave Martin contributed to the cardiac arrest
state_shift: Tommy has destroyed evidence linking him to a death he didn't cause, and the detective is now in the house
must_land_beats: Tommy's guilt about the Viagra — played for dark comedy, not melodrama
Tommy's clumsy tampering: taking the pills, dropping one in the hallway, flushing them
Mallick's arrival audible through the bathroom door — Tommy is trapped with evidence as the detective enters
Tommy's physicality under stress — pacing, gripping things, the coaching-mode body in a civilian crisis
secondary_character_beats: At least one moment where Tommy's coaching instincts surface inappropriately — he tries to manage the crisis like a halftime talk and it fails

payoffs_to_land.description: The joke from Ch 1 becomes Tommy's nightmare — he believes the Viagra triggered Martin's heart failure

pressure_source: Joel is the obvious suspect in a death that was natural causes
state_shift: Mallick is either an idiot or the smartest person in the house, and nobody can tell which
must_land_beats: Mallick's full entrance: casserole, wrong name (Kessner for Kessler), bathroom request before body
The guests correcting Mallick — each correction a small gift of information she catalogs
Joel's public defender instincts telling him to say nothing while his guilt about the argument pushes him to explain
Joel is the only one who didn't tamper — his clean hands look calculated, not innocent
Mallick's folksy performance should be detailed enough that the reader can't be sure it's an act
secondary_character_beats: Paul should be visibly torn between defending Martin and protecting the group
Nate should already be performing composure badly — the podcaster who can't stop narrating

setups_to_plant.description: Mallick introduces wrong information and the guests correct her — she thanks them each time and remembers everything. This mechanism will build across Ch 4-5 into the evidentiary map used in Ch 15-18

pressure_source: Mallick's interviews exposing more than the interviewees intend
state_shift: Mallick knows the scene was tampered with but not by how many people or why
must_land_beats: Paul corrects Mallick about the body's position — revealing he knows how it was oriented
Cliff corrects her about which room is Martin's study — revealing he was in there
Doug barely speaks in his pre-interview moment, and Mallick notes the silence
Mallick's private assessment: five men who claim they slept through the night, and the house was rearranged by morning
Each interview should feel distinct — Paul's insurance precision, Cliff's sales smoothness, Doug's monosyllables
secondary_character_beats: Mallick's domestic details (mentioning the casserole warming, asking about coffee) should function as disarming tools, not character tics

pressure_source: Nate's certainty that the deleted voicemail will be recovered and interpreted as a death threat
state_shift: Nate has essentially confessed to tampering while trying to explain why the tampering was innocent
must_land_beats: The voicemail content: Nate's riff on Martin's draft picks using death language that is now horrifying in context
Nate's attempt to verify the voicemail is truly deleted — checking Martin's phone, googling carrier retention policies
Nate's interrogation with Mallick: his compulsive need to talk, explain, narrate, contextualize
The moment Nate accidentally references the voicemail he claims not to have left
Mallick's quiet thank you for the correction
secondary_character_beats: Nate's podcaster instinct should be both comic and genuinely sad — this is how he processes everything, including grief

pressure_source: Doug's depression, divorce, and the shame of being medicated in front of his oldest friends
state_shift: Doug becomes the first character the reader sees as fully human outside the comedy — his vulnerability makes the later tonal break land
must_land_beats: Doug's medication routine — the antidepressant, the anti-anxiety med, the act of hiding bottles
His tampering was the most innocent: removing prescription bottles out of pure shame, not guilt
Doug's interview with Mallick: monosyllables that create more suspicion than Nate's verbal flood
A moment alone on the dock — Doug watching the lake, the comedy becoming gentler
Doug's self-deprecating humor should be genuine and warm, not a cry for help
secondary_character_beats: Doug's observation of the other men should be sharp and specific — his silence makes him the group's best witness, which is ironic given he saw the least

pressure_source: Cliff's financial desperation driving him to Martin's safe, where he finds something far more valuable than money
state_shift: Cliff holds the most dangerous secret in the house — leverage that he does not yet understand is lethal
must_land_beats: Cliff's financial motive: Martin owed him forty thousand from a failed joint real estate deal
The safe: wall-mounted, combination lock, Martin left the dial one tick off
The manila envelope labeled RT — Cliff expects real estate documents
Martin's handwritten account of the 2002 cabin fire in Washburn County, Wisconsin
The account names Tommy as the one who started the fire; names Ray Toomey as the caretaker who died
Cliff reads it twice, puts it back, and the reader should feel the temperature change
secondary_character_beats: Cliff's financial panic should be legible as genuine desperation, not greed — his business is failing and this money matters

setups_to_plant.description: The handwritten account of the 2002 fire — Martin's record of Tommy starting the fire that killed Ray Toomey. Cliff finds it, photographs it, and replaces it. This document drives the murder motive and the resolution.

pressure_source: the group demanding Joel explain his argument with Martin
state_shift: the group's loyalty to Martin splinters; Joel's motive becomes sympathetic; the group fractures along new lines
must_land_beats: The group pushes Joel to explain the argument — the pressure has been building since Ch 4
Joel reveals: Martin was sleeping with his ex-wife Karen
The group's reaction treated like a fantasy football ethics debate — was it a violation? Is there a statute of limitations?
Paul's conflict: defending Martin while knowing Martin was wrong
Joel's catharsis — his anger finally has an audience and it is both genuine and absurd
secondary_character_beats: Tommy should weigh in with coaching-mode moral certainty that rings hollow given what the reader now suspects
Doug should say almost nothing but his face should register something — he understands being betrayed by someone close

payoffs_to_land.description: The argument from Ch 1 is explained — Martin was sleeping with Joel's ex-wife Karen. Joel's apparent suspiciousness dissolves as his motive (rage at a dead man) becomes sympathetically legible.

pressure_source: the evidence from multiple tamperings forming a contradictory mess that no single theory explains
state_shift: Mallick realizes multiple people tampered independently — she is asking the right question but will reach the wrong conclusion about Martin's death and the right one about Cliff's
must_land_beats: Mallick's evidence review: body repositioned AND wiped, safe opened, voicemail deleted, medications missing — too scattered for one plan
The realization: this was a committee that never met, not a conspiracy
Mallick's comic imagination of five middle-aged men sneaking through the same house simultaneously
Her new theory: five panic attacks, not one murder — which is correct about Martin but creates the framework for solving Cliff's death later
A re-interview scene where Mallick tests the multi-tamperer theory against someone's composure
secondary_character_beats: Mallick should have a moment of genuine puzzlement that humanizes her beyond the methodical detective — she's amused and bewildered by the sheer absurdity

pressure_source: Paul's discovery that Martin's central loyalty was not to him but to Tommy
state_shift: Paul's grief shifts from loss to suspicion — Martin was hiding something about Tommy, and the will is proof it wasn't casual
must_land_beats: Paul in Martin's room going through his things — the intimacy and invasiveness of handling a dead friend's belongings
Paul finds Martin's will in the study: Martin left Tommy the lake house, not Paul
Memories of the Martin-Tommy dynamic across twenty-five years: protectiveness, tolerance, the way Martin always ensured Tommy came back
Paul begins to suspect a deeper bond — something that created obligation, not just friendship
The comedy in Paul's indignation should be real: he's also hurt that his best friend left the house to someone else
secondary_character_beats: Paul's memories of Martin should include at least one that is purely funny and purely kind — the reader needs to grieve Martin too

setups_to_plant.description: Martin left Tommy the lake house in his will. This fact, combined with the fire document, will be the emotional trigger in Paul's confrontation with Tommy in Ch 17.

pressure_source: Cliff leveraging the fire document against Tommy
state_shift: Tommy is under direct external threat — his guilt about the Viagra was internal and manageable; Cliff's leverage is external, ongoing, and escalating. The murder motive is fully formed.
must_land_beats: Cliff approaches Tommy on the deck with two beers and a proposition
Cliff names the fire, names Ray Toomey, references the document
Cliff's ask: not cash, but Tommy's influence to recover the forty thousand Martin owed
Tommy's flashback to the night of the fire — the truck, the smoke, twenty-two years of managed guilt
Tommy agrees to Cliff's terms but something physical shifts — the jock bluster the reader has been trained to read as comedy is now legible as genuine danger
The comedy in this chapter should have a visible edge that the reader can feel but might not identify as lethal
secondary_character_beats: Cliff's leverage play should feel like a real estate negotiation, not a villain's demand — he genuinely thinks he's being reasonable

payoffs_to_land.description: Cliff uses the document he discovered in Ch 8 to leverage Tommy. The financial motive that led Cliff to the safe has been replaced by a leverage play worth far more than forty thousand dollars.

pressure_source: the group's desperate performance of normalcy while every relationship is fractured
state_shift: the group's pretense of normalcy is audibly cracking — Nate observes the Tommy-Cliff tension — last comedy before the break
must_land_beats: The fantasy football waiver wire debate: should Martin's auto-drafted team stand? Who manages his roster as tribute?
The argument becomes a proxy for loyalty, obligation, and what they owe each other and the dead
Nate observes: Tommy and Cliff sitting too far apart and being too polite
A last dinner, a sunset on the lake — the final moment that feels like the weekends that came before
This chapter must land as genuinely funny while the reader senses, consciously or not, that the comedy is about to end
secondary_character_beats: Doug should be drinking water instead of beer — a small detail that matters later
Joel and Paul should exchange a look that signals they are beginning to compare notes

pressure_source: the second death — the real murder
state_shift: Cliff is dead. The comedy is over. Everything the reader laughed at in chapters 1-13 is reframed — the clues to the murder were in the comedy.
must_land_beats: The investigation into Martin's death winding down — Mallick leaning toward natural causes
Doug goes to the dock for his evening ritual — the same dock from his quiet moment in Ch 7
Doug finds Cliff floating face-down in the water beside the dock
The details that signal staging: Cliff's leather loafers instead of his deck shoes, the gash on his temple, shallow water
Doug's scream — the quietest man in the group makes the sound that ends the comedy
The doubled grief: Cliff's death and the death of the weekend as something survivable
secondary_character_beats: Doug's discovery should draw on the vulnerability established in Ch 7 — this is the man who came here because he needed people, and now he's finding a dead one
The group's arrival at the dock should show each man's specific shock — not a generic reaction

pressure_source: a real homicide with real suspects — Mallick without the folksy persona
state_shift: Mallick is investigating a real murder; the corrections from the comedy become the evidentiary map for the tragedy
must_land_beats: Mallick returns: no casserole, no wrong names — she gets Martin's name right and the guests notice
The house locked down as a crime scene, forensics called
Mallick examines Cliff's body: head wound inconsistent with a fall, water in lungs indicating submersion, wrong shoes
Mallick applies her correction map from the Martin investigation to Cliff's death — who had access, motive, opportunity
Her private assessment: someone strong, desperate, and careless enough to get the shoes wrong
The contrast between this Mallick and casserole Mallick should be stark and earned
secondary_character_beats: Each surviving guest's reaction to the new Mallick should be distinct — Joel recognizes the real detective, Tommy recognizes the danger, Nate can't stop talking, Doug goes silent, Paul tries to help

payoffs_to_land.description: The correction map Mallick built during the comic investigation — who knew which rooms, who corrected which details, who was where — now becomes the operational tool for solving Cliff's murder.

Nate's podcast recordings from draft night and subsequent evenings contain ambient audio — footsteps, voices, timing — that helps Mallick build a timeline. The recordings Nate made for content become evidence.

pressure_source: Joel and Paul assembling the full picture from civilian evidence
state_shift: Joel and Paul know Tommy killed Cliff — the question is what they do with it
must_land_beats: Joel and Paul on the deck comparing notes — Paul's suspicion about Martin-Tommy, Joel's observations about Tommy-Cliff
They go to the study and find the fire document in the safe — the envelope has been unfolded twice by different hands
Joel reads Martin's account of the 2002 fire and Ray Toomey's death
The picture assembles: Cliff found the document, Cliff leveraged Tommy, Tommy killed Cliff
Joel and Paul must decide: go to Mallick, confront Tommy, or protect the group one last time the way Martin would have
Joel's legal mind and Paul's loyalty to Martin's memory should pull in opposite directions
secondary_character_beats: Joel's public defender instincts should make him aware of what this evidence means legally — and what it doesn't prove without confession

payoffs_to_land.description: Joel and Paul find and read the fire document that Cliff discovered in Ch 8 and used as leverage in Ch 12. The crease pattern reveals it was handled by multiple people. The full picture of the murder motive is now assembled.

pressure_source: Paul confronting Tommy with the truth about the fire, the leverage, and Cliff's murder
state_shift: Tommy's secret is out between them — Paul decides not to protect Tommy the way Martin did
must_land_beats: Paul confronts Tommy alone — with grief, not aggression
Martin spent twenty-two years protecting Tommy, and now Martin is dead and Cliff is dead and the protection is gone
Tommy's response: denial, then anger, then something that looks like relief at being known
Paul tells Tommy that Martin left him the house — this is the detail that breaks Tommy
The irony: the chain of obligation was also the thing holding Tommy together
Paul's ultimatum: Tommy has until morning to go to Mallick, or Paul will
secondary_character_beats: Tommy's breakdown should not be theatrical — a man sitting on a deck realizing his life is over, not a dramatic confession

payoffs_to_land.description: The subtle protectiveness seeded across the novel resolves here — Martin's loyalty to Tommy was not friendship but managed guilt and genuine, complicated love. The will, the protectiveness, the twenty-five years of making sure Tommy came back — all of it was about the fire.
Paul tells Tommy that Martin left him the house. Tommy breaks — the gift is also a sentence, proof that Martin's entire relationship with Tommy was defined by the fire.

pressure_source: Tommy's decision and Mallick's final assembly of the case
state_shift: the mystery resolves — Martin died of natural causes, Cliff was murdered to protect a secret Martin's death set loose, and the league is over
must_land_beats: Tommy comes to Mallick in the kitchen at 6 AM
Tommy tells her about Cliff — the dock, the push, the water — but not about the fire (legal instinct)
Mallick reads him his rights; the other men watch from the great room
Tommy taken out in handcuffs
Mallick alone in the kitchen — the casserole still in the fridge, untouched
The fantasy football draft board still on the wall with Martin's picks circled in red
The league is over — the final image should be quiet and earned
secondary_character_beats: The surviving men watching Tommy leave should each register differently: Joel the lawyer, Paul the best friend, Nate the narrator, Doug the witness
Mallick's final moment alone should carry the weight of someone who solves crimes for a living and is still affected by this one

payoffs_to_land.description: Mallick's full evidentiary map — built from the comedy of corrections across the novel — underpins the case against Tommy. The reader realizes the detective was building the case while appearing to bumble.
